label: ai
topic: The competitive pressure at an elite university causes students to lose their personal identity
text:
There's a certain look people give you on campus, and once you notice it, you can't unsee it. It's the tight smile, the slightly too-quick "I'm good," the carefully curated balance between stressed and composed. At my university, we call it "Penn Face," but it's not really about one school. It's a culture, a performance, a quiet agreement that no matter how overwhelmed you feel, you don't let it show. You keep it together, at least on the surface.

I didn't realize how much I'd internalized that until I started losing track of who I was underneath it. Somewhere between back-to-back classes, extracurriculars, and the constant pressure to be "doing enough," my sense of identity got replaced by a checklist. What internship am I applying for? What club am I leading? What impressive thing can I say when someone asks what I've been up to? Conversations started to feel less like genuine exchanges and more like subtle competitions, each of us presenting the most polished version of our lives.

The strange thing is that everyone seems to know this is happening, and yet we keep participating in it. You'll sit with someone at 2 a.m., both of you exhausted, and there's this unspoken understanding that you're struggling—but instead of saying it outright, you make a joke about being "so busy" or "barely surviving." It's vulnerability, but filtered, diluted just enough to keep things from getting too real. We acknowledge the pressure without ever fully confronting it.

For me, the breaking point came in a moment that wasn't dramatic at all. I was sitting in the library, staring at a screen, trying to work on something I didn't care about anymore, and it hit me that I couldn't remember the last time I'd done something just because I wanted to. Everything had become instrumental, a means to an end. Even my interests started to feel strategic, chosen not because they mattered to me, but because they looked good on paper.

That realization was uncomfortable, but it was also clarifying. It made me start questioning why we accept this culture as normal. Why is it easier to admit that you haven't slept in two days than to admit that you're not okay? Why do we treat burnout like a badge of honor instead of a warning sign? The more I thought about it, the more it felt like we were all trapped in the same cycle, reinforcing it for each other without meaning to.

What I've started to realize is that breaking out of that cycle doesn't require some grand, campus-wide shift. It starts with smaller, more personal choices. Saying "I'm actually really stressed" instead of defaulting to "I'm fine." Choosing to spend time on something that doesn't have a clear payoff. Letting yourself be seen as something other than perfectly put together. These are small acts, but they push against the culture in meaningful ways.

I'm not naïve enough to think that vulnerability alone can fix a system that rewards overwork and comparison. But I do think it can create space for something different. When someone is honest about struggling, it gives other people permission to do the same. It shifts the tone of conversations, even if just a little. And over time, those small shifts can add up.

What I miss most about who I used to be isn't a specific activity or interest—it's the feeling of being grounded in something that was actually mine. Reclaiming that isn't easy, especially in an environment that constantly pulls you in other directions. But it starts with being honest, both with yourself and with the people around you. Because as polished as "Penn Face" might look from the outside, it's exhausting to maintain. And at some point, you have to ask whether it's worth it.
